md: An **angle** is what you get when two rays share a common endpoint — the **vertex**. The two rays are the **sides** of the angle. Angles are measured in **degrees**: a complete revolution is 360°.
**Right**: exactly 90°. We mark right angles with a small square.
**Straight**: exactly 180° — the two rays form a single straight line.
Drag the slider below — watch the type label change as you cross 90°, 180°, 270°.
**Complementary angles** sum to **90°**. If two angles are complementary, each one is the *complement* of the other. Example: 35° and 55° are complementary.
**Supplementary angles** sum to **180°**. Each is the *supplement* of the other. Example: 70° and 110° are supplementary.
**Vertical angles** are formed when two lines cross — the two pairs of *opposite* angles are always equal.

blocks.tex: \text{If } \alpha + \beta = 90°, \text{ they are complementary.} \\ \text{If } \alpha + \beta = 180°, \text{ they are supplementary.}

blocks.title: Memory hooks
blocks.body: **C**omplementary → **C**orner (90° corner). **S**upplementary → **S**traight line (180°). Look at the first letter of each.

md: **Two angles are vertical and one measures 71°.** Vertical angles are equal — the other is also 71°.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Five types: acute (<90°), right (=90°), obtuse (90°–180°), straight (=180°), reflex (>180°). Complementary pairs sum to 90°; supplementary pairs sum to 180°. Vertical angles (formed by intersecting lines) are always equal.
